content:
The adhesion kinetics reviewed above quantify degradation rates at specific interfaces, yet a pressing practical question remains: which encapsulant chemistry best resists adhesion loss under realistic thermal and humid field conditions? Answering this requires head-to-head comparison of the dominant commercial materials—ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA), polyolefin elastomer (POE), and ethylene-propylene-ethylene (EPE)—under controlled accelerated aging protocols.

Coupon-level thermal aging studies establish a clear ranking: POE encapsulant coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE and then EVA [REF:9]. This hierarchy aligns with the known crosslink density and moisture-barrier advantages of polyolefin-based chemistries relative to EVA, whose acetate side groups are susceptible to hydrolytic cleavage at elevated temperature. When extended aging at 5000 hours is applied under both hot-dry (90 °C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90 °C, 60% RH) conditions, all three materials—EVA, POE, and EPE—exhibit measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:2]. The universality of this decline across chemistries underscores that no current commercial encapsulant is immune to prolonged thermo-hygroscopic stress, though the magnitude of degradation differs by material and exposure environment.

A notable discordance emerges, however, when damp heat peel force data at the module level are examined alongside the coupon-level thermal aging results. EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE samples [REF:10], a reversal of the coupon-level thermal ranking in which POE outperformed EVA [REF:9]. This apparent contradiction likely reflects differences in test geometry, lamination conditions, and the distinct degradation mechanisms activated by humidity versus dry heat. Higher lamination temperature was shown to be beneficial for both peel force and gel content in cross-linking encapsulants before and after aging [REF:10], suggesting that manufacturing process parameters modulate durability at least as strongly as intrinsic polymer chemistry. These cross-source findings [REF:9][REF:10] highlight that encapsulant ranking is condition-dependent: POE excels under dry thermal stress, while EVA may retain interfacial adhesion more effectively when moisture is the dominant degrading agent.

| Metric | EVA | POE | EPE |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|
| Coupon adhesion stability (thermal aging) | Lowest [REF:9] | Best [REF:9] | Intermediate [REF:9] |
| Gc after 5000 h hot-dry (90 °C, ~1% RH) | Decreased [REF:2] | Decreased [REF:2] | Decreased [REF:2] |
| Gc after 5000 h hot-humid (90 °C, 60% RH) | Decreased [REF:2] | Decreased [REF:2] | Decreased [REF:2] |
| Peel force degradation after DH (module level) | Less degradation [REF:10] | More degradation [REF:10] | Not tested |
| Benefit of higher lamination temperature | Yes (cross-linking) [REF:10] | Not reported | Not reported |

Notably absent from the available evidence is any direct adhesion data for thermoplastic polyolefin (TPO) encapsulants under damp heat, despite growing commercial interest in TPO as a non-crosslinking alternative. Similarly, quantitative Gc values, statistical confidence intervals, and sample sizes are not reported in the available claims, limiting the ability to assess effect magnitudes or perform formal meta-comparison. The failure mode transition from interfacial to combined cohesive-interfacial failure—referenced in the broader literature on POE—lacks quantitative support within the current claim set, representing a gap that targeted peel testing with fractographic analysis could address.

**Key Findings**

- POE encapsulant coupons rank highest in adhesion stability under thermal aging, followed by EPE and then EVA [REF:9].
- All three encapsulants (EVA, POE, EPE) show decreased adhesion energy Gc after 5000 hours of both hot-dry and hot-humid aging at 90 °C [REF:2].
- At the module level under damp heat, EVA laminates exhibited less peel force degradation than POE, reversing the coupon-level thermal ranking, with higher lamination temperature proving beneficial for cross-linking encapsulants [REF:10][REF:9].
- No adhesion data under damp heat conditions are available for TPO encapsulants in the current evidence base.
- Encapsulant ranking is condition-dependent: dry thermal stress favors POE, while damp heat performance may favor EVA at the module level [REF:9][REF:10].
